food_subgroup: Whole mud crab (Scylla serrata) cooked in tomato-egg-chilli gravy, served with fried mantou buns

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Singapore Chilli Crab. Closest defensible parent is cooked crab dish (FOODON_03315672). A more specific term would require a new FoodOn class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crab, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No FoodEx2 code exists for the composite Singapore Chilli Crab dish including gravy and mantou.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Singapore Chilli Crab as a composite dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food composition codes for regional composite dishes.

serving.description: 1 full meal serving: 1 whole mud crab (~1.2 kg total weight, ~360–400 g edible portion) + ~200 g gravy + 2 fried mantou (~100 g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving of Singapore Chilli Crab at a seafood restaurant consists of one whole mud crab (approximately 1.2 kg live weight yielding 360–400 g edible meat), approximately 200 g of tomato-egg-chilli gravy, and 2 fried mantou buns (~100 g), totalling approximately 450 g of consumed food and 700–900 kcal per person.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Crab — Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo / HPB Nutrient Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Singapore Chilli Crab (crab + gravy composite). Micronutrient values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, blue crab cooked, USDA #15143) as HPB does not report these fields. The composite per-100g profile represents the edible crab meat plus gravy fraction; the fried mantou contribution is captured in the serving-level GL calculation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for composite seafood-sauce dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Chilli Crab as a composite dish. GI ~55 (low) is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: tomato-based sauce with added sugar and cornstarch (GI ~55–60 range for tomato-based sauces with starch thickening, per University of Sydney GI Database analogues), moderated by the high protein and fat content of crab meat and eggs. The fried mantou (GI ~65, white bread analogue) contributes the dominant carbohydrate load at the meal level. Glycemic load per full meal serving (450 g, ~31 g total carbohydrate including mantou) is calculated as (55 × 31) / 100 = ~17 for the crab+gravy fraction plus ~3 for the mantou fraction, totalling approximately 20 GL units per meal serving. Per-100g GL = (55 × 5) / 100 = 2.75, rounded to 2.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 300 mg per 100 g of crab-plus-gravy composite, with a full meal serving delivering approximately 900 mg sodium from gravy, soy sauce, sambal, and fried mantou combined
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Singapore Chilli Crab to no more than one occasion per month, request reduced sauce, avoid dipping mantou in the gravy, and refrain from adding extra soy sauce or sambal at the table, as the gravy and mantou together account for the majority of the meal's sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from cornstarch- and sugar-thickened gravy, with the dominant glycemic concern being the 2 fried mantou buns (~26 g refined carbohydrate at GI ~65) that accompany the dish, raising total meal GL to approximately 20 units
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit mantou to 1 steamed bun instead of 2 fried buns to reduce meal GL by approximately 8–10 units, consume the crab meat preferentially over the gravy, and monitor post-meal blood glucose as the combined refined-starch and sugar load of the full serving is clinically significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content approximately 120 mg per 100 g from crab meat and roe composite, with additional cholesterol from egg in the gravy; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.5 g per 100 g, but the full meal sodium load (~900 mg) and deep-fried mantou fat content are the primary cardiovascular concerns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease may consume Singapore Chilli Crab occasionally (no more than twice per month) by choosing steamed mantou over fried, limiting gravy intake to reduce sodium and refined-carbohydrate exposure, and avoiding the hepatopancreas ('crab butter') which contributes an additional 150–200 mg cholesterol per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Excellent zinc (4.5 mg/100 g), selenium (35 mcg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (3.5 mcg/100 g) content, all clinically relevant for immune regulation and methotrexate users
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Crab meat is nutritionally beneficial for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high zinc, selenium, and B12 content; the primary caution is the sodium load from the gravy, which should be minimised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Chilli Crab is the city-state's most iconic seafood dish and is widely regarded as Singapore's national dish. It consists of whole mud crab (Scylla serrata) that is halved, wok-fried, and then simmered in a semi-thick tomato-egg-chilli gravy made from tomato purée, sambal chilli paste, beaten eggs swirled into the sauce to create ribbon-like strands, cornstarch for thickening, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sugar. The gravy is characteristically orange-red, tangy, mildly sweet, and moderately spicy. The dish is traditionally served with fried or steamed mantou buns used to mop up the sauce. The dish was created in the 1950s, commonly attributed to Cher Yam Tian, who sold it from a pushcart along River Valley Road around 1956. A full restaurant serving — one whole crab (~1.2 kg live weight, ~360–400 g edible meat), ~200 g gravy, and 2 fried mantou — totals approximately 700–900 kcal per person. Nutritionally, the crab meat itself is an excellent lean protein source (13 g protein per 100 g), rich in zinc (4.5 mg), selenium (35 mcg), and vitamin B12 (3.5 mcg), making it beneficial for immune function and neurological health. The gravy contributes sodium (~300 mg/100 g), refined carbohydrate from cornstarch and sugar, and cholesterol from egg. The fried mantou is the dominant calorie and carbohydrate vehicle in the meal. Clinically, the primary concerns are the high sodium load (~900 mg per full meal serving) relevant to hypertension, and the glycemic contribution of the mantou (GI ~65) relevant to Type 2 Diabetes management. Switching to 1 steamed mantou reduces the meal by approximately 120 kcal and 8 g fat. Halal status: NOT halal — mud crab (Scylla serrata) is not halal-certified in Singapore. The dish is dairy-free and contains allergens: crustacean, egg, gluten (mantou), and soy.

mud_crab: flower crab (different species but sometimes confused)

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (blue crab cooked) as HPB does not report these fields; these three fields carry confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate documented with University of Sydney GI Database analogues. All other values are directly supported by the HPB Singapore source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Singapore Chilli Crab composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite crab-plus-gravy dish is not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source for this preparation). 4. Copper (0.08 mg/100g), manganese (0.12 mg/100g), and vitamin K (1.2 mcg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked blue crab, USDA #15143) as the closest approved-source analogue; HPB Singapore does not report these micronutrients for this dish. 5. The per-100g profile represents the crab meat plus gravy composite fraction only; the fried mantou contribution is accounted for at the serving level in the GL calculation. 6. Halal status: NOT halal — mud crab (Scylla serrata) is not halal-certified in Singapore. 7. The hepatopancreas ('crab butter', orange roe-like organ) contributes an additional 150–200 mg cholesterol if consumed and is not included in the standard per-100g composite. 8. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Singapore Chilli Crab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 9. FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crab, cooked) used as closest defensible parent; no composite dish code exists in FoodEx2 for this preparation.
